article_title: Global Wind and Solar Additions Hit Record 814 GW in 2025, Pushing Total Capacity Past the 4 Terawatt Mark

content_preview.title: Global Wind and Solar Additions Hit Record 814 GW in 2025, Pushing Total Capacity Past the 4 Terawatt Mark
content_preview.summary: The world installed a record 814 GW of wind and solar capacity in 2025, a 17 percent increase over 2024, lifting total global capacity past 4 terawatts for the first time while wind and solar overtook fossil fuels in EU electricity generation.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The world added a record 814 gigawatts of new wind and solar capacity in 2025, a 17 percent increase over the 696 GW installed in 2024, according to data published by energy think tank Ember and reported by [Electrek](https://electrek.co/2026/03/19/the-world-added-a-record-814-gw-wind-solar/). Total installed wind and solar capacity now stands at 4,174 GW, crossing the 4 terawatt threshold for the first time. In a parallel milestone, wind and solar generated more electricity than fo...

source_verification.findings: Confirms 814 GW record, 17% increase over 2024's 696 GW, total capacity 4,174 GW. Confirms solar 647 GW (11% increase), wind 167 GW (47% increase), 4:1 solar-to-wind ratio. Confirms Kingsmill Bond quote about energy security. Confirms 1,046 TWh annual generation potential. Note: the accessible article summary did NOT confirm China's two-thirds share claim attributed to this source — this figure may be in the full article or from Ember's underlying data.

Confirms wind and solar at 30% of EU electricity vs fossil fuels at 29%. Confirms five countries with solar exceeding 20% (Hungary, Cyprus, Greece, Spain, Netherlands). Confirms Ireland and Finland closed last coal plants in 2025. Confirms coal at less than 5% in 19 European countries. Confirms gas generation increased to compensate for drought-reduced hydro.

Confirms 30% EU electricity from wind/solar vs fossil fuels. Confirms 72 TWh curtailed in 2024 due to grid congestion. Confirms 584 billion euros grid investment needed by 2030. Confirms solar generation grew 20%+ for fourth consecutive year to 369 TWh. Confirms wind/solar share grew from 20% to 30% over five years while fossil fuels declined from 37% to 29%.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core statistics verified across all three sources: 814 GW record, 4 TW milestone, EU 30/29 split, grid investment needs. The China two-thirds share claim attributed to Electrek could not be confirmed from the accessible article content but is consistent with well-established Ember data trends. The 8% gas generation increase and 12% hydro drop attributed to Yale E360 are consistent with the source's reporting on gas compensating for drought-reduced hydro, though exact percentages could not be confirmed from the summary.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and data-driven throughout. Headline is factual without sensationalism. The Analysis section appropriately distinguishes between capacity additions and actual emissions impact. Good balance between the positive milestone and the grid infrastructure challenges.
editor_notes.originality: Effective synthesis of global Ember data with EU-specific analysis from two different sources. The juxtaposition of record installations with grid curtailment costs (72 TWh, 9 billion euros) provides important nuance. Cross-reference to prior IEA coverage adds continuity.
concerns: China's two-thirds share of global solar/wind additions is attributed to Electrek but could not be confirmed from the accessible article content. The figure may be in the full article or sourced from Ember's data directly.
Word count (814) is at the upper boundary of the News category range (400-1200) but acceptable.
The 8% gas generation increase and 12% hydro drop figures attributed to Yale E360 are directionally consistent with the source but exact percentages not confirmed in summary.
recommendations: China-specific claims would be strengthened by citing Ember's data report directly.
The gas/hydro percentage figures should be verified against the full Yale E360 article to confirm exact numbers.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong energy/climate news article with well-sourced data from Ember (via Electrek), Yale E360, and Euronews. The combination of global milestone coverage with EU-specific analysis and grid infrastructure challenges provides comprehensive context. Minor concerns about specific China and gas/hydro figures do not undermine the article's overall accuracy.
